display.title: Supreme Court decision-making in slavery cases
display.publisher: University of Southern California Digital Library
display.publicationTitle: University of Southern California Digital Library
display.journalAbbreviation: University of Southern California Digital Library
display.abstract: This paper looks at Supreme Court decision-making by examining cases that involved issues of slavery. After developing a population of 14 cases, I test the decisions in light of the three alternative hypotheses: attitudinalism, legalism, and regime theory. Each theory explains a different set of cases. The attitudinal model captures cases involving Mississippi laws governing slavery. The legal model explains suits in which the issue was whether a black was a slave or not. Finally, the regime account is most important in fugitive slave law.
